subject: ap-calculus-bc
theme: Integration reverses differentiation — the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus connects antiderivatives to definite integrals, and BC adds integration by parts, partial fractions, and improper integrals.

core_concepts.title: FTC Part 1 (Accumulation)
core_concepts.detail: If $F(x) = \int_a^x f(t)\,dt$, then $F'(x) = f(x)$. With Chain Rule: $\frac{d}{dx}\int_a^{g(x)} f(t)\,dt = f(g(x)) \cdot g'(x)$.

core_concepts.title: FTC Part 2 (Evaluation)
core_concepts.detail: $\int_a^b f(x)\,dx = F(b) - F(a)$ where $F$ is any antiderivative of $f$. This converts area under a curve to antiderivative evaluation.

core_concepts.title: u-Substitution
core_concepts.detail: Reverse Chain Rule: set $u = g(x)$, $du = g'(x)\,dx$, then $\int f(g(x))g'(x)\,dx = \int f(u)\,du$. For definite integrals, change the limits to $u$-values.

core_concepts.title: Integration by Parts (BC)
core_concepts.detail: $\int u\,dv = uv - \int v\,du$. Choose $u$ using LIATE (Log, Inverse trig, Algebraic, Trig, Exponential). May need to apply twice for integrals like $\int e^x \sin x\,dx$.

core_concepts.title: Partial Fractions (BC)
core_concepts.detail: Decompose $\frac{P(x)}{Q(x)}$ into simpler fractions when $Q$ factors into distinct linear terms: $\frac{1}{(x-a)(x-b)} = \frac{A}{x-a} + \frac{B}{x-b}$. Then integrate each term.

core_concepts.title: Improper Integrals (BC)
core_concepts.detail: $\int_a^{\infty} f(x)\,dx = \lim_{b \to \infty} \int_a^b f(x)\,dx$. If the limit exists (finite), the integral converges; otherwise it diverges. Also applies to integrands with vertical asymptotes.

core_concepts.title: Riemann Sums
core_concepts.detail: Left, right, midpoint, and trapezoidal sums approximate $\int_a^b f(x)\,dx$. Left/right overestimate depends on monotonicity; trapezoidal overestimates when concave up.

formulas.name: FTC Part 1
formulas.description: The derivative of an accumulation function gives back the integrand. With a function as the upper limit, apply the Chain Rule.

formulas.name: FTC Part 2
formulas.description: Evaluate a definite integral by finding any antiderivative F and computing the difference at the endpoints.

formulas.name: Integration by Parts
formulas.description: BC-only technique for integrating products. Choose u to be the function that simplifies when differentiated (LIATE order).
formulas.example: $\int x e^x\,dx$: let $u = x$, $dv = e^x\,dx$. Then $uv - \int v\,du = xe^x - \int e^x\,dx = xe^x - e^x + C$.

formulas.name: Improper Integral
formulas.description: Replace the infinite bound with a variable b, evaluate the definite integral, then take the limit. If the limit is finite, the integral converges.

formulas.name: Trapezoidal Rule
formulas.description: Approximates a definite integral using trapezoids. More accurate than left/right sums for the same number of subintervals.
formulas.example: Approximate $\int_0^2 x^2\,dx$ with $n = 2$: $\frac{1}{2}[f(0) + 2f(1) + f(2)] = \frac{1}{2}[0 + 2 + 4] = 3$. Exact value: $\frac{8}{3} \approx 2.667$.

exam_tip: On BC, you must know integration by parts and partial fractions — these are tested directly. For improper integrals, always write the limit notation; jumping straight to the answer without the limit loses points on FRQs.
